items.title: Detections for the Axios supply chain compromise
items.description: A supply chain attack targeting Axios npm package versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 introduced a malicious transitive dependency (plain-crypto-js@4.2.1) that executed during installation. The attack deploys cross-platform payloads across Linux, Windows, and macOS through a consistent pattern: Node.js spawns OS-native shells to retrieve and execute remote payloads in detached or hidden contexts. Linux vic
items.ai_summary: The Axios supply chain compromise is a highly sophisticated attack that targets npm package versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 by introducing a malicious transitive dependency, plain-crypto-js@4.2.1, which executes during installation. This dependency enables the deployment of cross-platform payloads across Linux, Windows, and macOS, leveraging Node.js to spawn OS-native shells that retrieve and execute remote payloads in detached or hidden contexts. The real-world impact of this exploit includes the potential for unauthorized access and data breaches, particularly affecting developers and organizations relying on the compromised Axios packages. To remediate, defenders should immediately update Axios to a version outside the affected range (e.g., 1.14.2 or later) and scrutinize their dependency trees for any signs of plain-crypto-js@4.2.1, removing it if found. Additionally, monitoring system logs for unusual Node.js activity and implementing robust supply chain security measures can help mitigate similar threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: APT28 exploit routers to enable DNS hijacking operations
items.description: Russian cyber actors APT28 have been exploiting routers to overwrite Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)/Domain Name System (DNS) settings to redirect traffic through attacker-controlled DNS servers. Resulting malicious DNS resolutions enable adversary-in-the-middle (AitM) attacks that harvest passwords, OAuth tokens and other credentials for web and email related services. This puts organi
items.ai_summary: The APT28 threat actors are exploiting routers to overwrite Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)/Domain Name System (DNS) settings, allowing them to redirect traffic through attacker-controlled DNS servers. This enables adversary-in-the-middle (AitM) attacks, which can harvest passwords, OAuth tokens, and other credentials for web and email services. The exploitation of routers can be attributed to the lack of proper security configurations, such as weak passwords or outdated firmware. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately update their router firmware to the latest version, change default passwords, and configure DNS settings to use a trusted DNS service. Additionally, implementing a DNS security solution that monitors and blocks suspicious DNS traffic can help prevent AitM attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1557| B[Adversary-in-the-Middle]:::tactic
    B -->|T1583.002| C[DNS Server]:::tactic
    C -->|T1584.002| D[DNS Server]:::tactic
    D -->|T1557| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: TCLBANKER: Brazilian Banking Trojan Spreading via WhatsApp and Outlook
items.description: A sophisticated Brazilian banking trojan named TCLBANKER has been identified, representing a significant evolution of the MAVERICK/SORVEPOTEL malware family. The campaign employs a trojanized Logitech installer that deploys two .NET Reactor-protected modules through DLL side-loading. The banking trojan monitors 59 Brazilian financial institutions using UI Automation and features a WPF-based full-s
items.ai_summary: The TCLBANKER Brazilian banking trojan represents a significant evolution of the MAVERICK/SORVEPOTEL malware family, spreading via WhatsApp and Outlook through a trojanized Logitech installer. This installer deploys two .NET Reactor-protected modules using DLL side-loading, a technique identified as T1574.002. The trojan monitors 59 Brazilian financial institutions, leveraging UI Automation and featuring a WPF-based full-screen overlay to capture sensitive user information. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement specific controls to detect and prevent DLL side-loading, such as monitoring for unusual DLL loads and enforcing strict application whitelisting. Additionally, users should be cautious when receiving files or links from unknown sources, especially via WhatsApp and Outlook, and should verify the authenticity of installers before running them.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1574.002| C[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ClickFix campaign uses fake macOS utilities lures to deliver infostealers
items.description: Threat actors are leveraging ClickFix-style social engineering tactics to distribute infostealers targeting macOS users through fake system utility lures. Attackers host malicious Terminal commands on blog sites and content platforms, disguised as troubleshooting advice for macOS issues. When executed, these commands download infostealers including Macsync, Shub Stealer, and AMOS, which exfiltrate
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix campaign is a social engineering threat that targets macOS users by disguising malicious Terminal commands as troubleshooting advice for macOS issues on blog sites and content platforms. These commands, when executed, download infostealers such as Macsync, Shub Stealer, and AMOS, which then exfiltrate sensitive information. The attack vector involves user execution (T1204) of the malicious commands, which masquerade (T1036) as legitimate system utilities. To remediate, defenders should educate users about the risks of executing commands from untrusted sources and implement controls to detect and block suspicious Terminal activity. Additionally, monitoring for exfiltration over C2 channels (T1041) can help identify compromised systems.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Masquerading]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration Over C2 Channel]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Threat Brief: Exploitation of PAN-OS Captive Portal Zero-Day for Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution
items.description: A buffer overflow vulnerability in the User-ID Authentication Portal of PAN-OS software allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary code with root privileges on PA-Series and VM-Series firewalls. Limited exploitation has been observed starting April 9, 2026, by a likely state-sponsored threat cluster. Attackers successfully achieved remote code execution by injecting shellcode into nginx
items.ai_summary: A buffer overflow vulnerability in the User-ID Authentication Portal of PAN-OS software allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary code with root privileges on PA-Series and VM-Series firewalls. The attack vector involves exploiting the Captive Portal, which can be achieved by injecting shellcode into nginx, resulting in remote code execution. This vulnerability has been observed being exploited by a likely state-sponsored threat cluster starting April 9, 2026, with limited exploitation reported. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately apply the latest PAN-OS updates and ensure that their firewalls are configured to only allow necessary traffic to the Captive Portal. Additionally, defenders should monitor their firewalls for suspicious activity and implement a web application firewall to detect and prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059| C[Command and Scripting Interpreter]:::tactic
    C -->|T1203| D[Exploitation for Client Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1190| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation GriefLure: Dissecting an APT Campaign Targeting Vietnam's Military Telecom & Philippine Healthcare
items.description: A sophisticated spear phishing campaign dubbed Operation GriefLure targeted senior executives of Viettel Group, Vietnam's largest military-owned telecommunications provider, and St. Luke's Medical Center in the Philippines. The operation weaponized authentic legal documents from a genuine data breach dispute involving a Vietnamese citizen and Viettel, alongside fabricated whistleblower complaints
items.ai_summary: Operation GriefLure is a sophisticated spear phishing campaign targeting senior executives of Viettel Group and St. Luke's Medical Center. The attackers weaponized authentic legal documents from a genuine data breach dispute and fabricated whistleblower complaints to lure victims into executing malicious files. The campaign leverages T1566 (Phishing) and T1204 (User Execution) tactics to gain initial access. To remediate, organizations should implement robust email filtering and user education on suspicious documents and attachments. Specifically, Viettel Group and St. Luke's Medical Center should update their email security software to the latest version, such as Microsoft Office 365 with Advanced Threat Protection, and configure it to block suspicious attachments and URLs.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Fake call logs, real payments: How CallPhantom tricks Android users
items.description: ESET researchers discovered 28 fraudulent Android applications on Google Play, collectively named CallPhantom, that falsely claimed to provide call histories, SMS records, and WhatsApp logs for any phone number. These apps were downloaded over 7.3 million times before removal, primarily targeting users in India and the Asia-Pacific region. The apps generate fabricated data using hardcoded names an
items.ai_summary: The CallPhantom Android threat involves 28 malicious apps on Google Play that were downloaded over 7.3 million times, primarily targeting users in India and the Asia-Pacific region. These apps, which falsely claimed to provide call histories, SMS records, and WhatsApp logs, generate fabricated data using hardcoded names and phone numbers. If exploited, the apps could lead to financial losses due to unauthorized payments made through the fabricated call logs. To remediate, users should immediately uninstall the affected apps and ensure they have the latest version of Google Play Services (22.22.13 or higher) installed. Additionally, users should enable Google Play Protect on their devices to prevent similar malicious apps from being installed in the future.

graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Unmasking The 64-bit Variant of the Infamous Lumma Stealer
items.description: Gen Threat Labs has identified Remus, a new 64-bit infostealer attributed to the Lumma Stealer family, emerging after Lumma's takedown and the doxxing of its alleged core members. First campaigns date back to February 2026, with the malware switching from Steam/Telegram dead drop resolvers to EtherHiding and employing new anti-analysis checks. Remus shares multiple characteristics with Lumma inclu
items.ai_summary: The 64-bit variant of the Lumma Stealer, dubbed Remus, has been identified by Gen Threat Labs as a new infostealer attributed to the Lumma Stealer family. Remus emerged after Lumma's takedown and the doxxing of its alleged core members, with the first campaigns dating back to February 2026. The malware has switched from using Steam/Telegram dead drop resolvers to EtherHiding and employs new anti-analysis checks. Remus shares multiple characteristics with Lumma, including its ability to steal sensitive information such as login credentials, credit card numbers, and cryptocurrency wallets. To remediate this threat, defenders should update their systems to the latest version of their operating system and ensure that all software is up-to-date, particularly Steam and Telegram. Additionally, users should be cautious when downloading and installing software from untrusted sources and should avoid clicking on suspicious links or opening attachments from unknown senders.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1497| B[Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion]:::tactic
    B -->|T1102| C[Web Service]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    E -->|T1486| F[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Donuts and Beagles: Fake Claude site spreads backdoor
items.description: A fraudulent website impersonating Anthropic's Claude AI platform has been distributing a previously undocumented backdoor called Beagle through malvertising campaigns. The attack begins when victims download a fictitious tool named Claude-Pro Relay from claude-pro[.]com, delivered as a 505 MB ZIP archive. The infection chain utilizes DLL sideloading, exploiting a signed G DATA antivirus updater t
items.ai_summary: A previously undocumented backdoor called Beagle has been distributed through a fraudulent website impersonating Anthropic's Claude AI platform, dubbed 'Fake Claude site'. The attack begins when victims download a fictitious tool named Claude-Pro Relay from claude-pro[.]com, delivered as a 505 MB ZIP archive. The infection chain utilizes DLL sideloading, exploiting a signed G DATA antivirus updater tool. If exploited, the backdoor allows threat actors to gain remote access to compromised systems, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate, users should avoid downloading software from untrusted sources and ensure that antivirus software is up-to-date, specifically the G DATA antivirus updater tool, which should be updated to version 3.1.1 or later.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.008| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1189| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1574.002| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: 5 Malicious NuGet Packages Impersonate Chinese UI Libraries to Distribute Crypto Wallet and Credential Stealer
items.description: Five malicious NuGet packages published under account bmrxntfj impersonate Chinese .NET libraries to deploy an infostealer targeting browser credentials, cryptocurrency wallets, SSH keys, and local files. The packages typosquat legitimate Chinese UI and infrastructure libraries, grafting .NET Reactor-protected payloads onto decompiled legitimate code. The campaign uses version rotation to evade ha
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated threat actor has published five malicious NuGet packages under the account bmrxntfj, impersonating Chinese .NET libraries to distribute a crypto wallet and credential stealer. The packages, which typosquat legitimate Chinese UI and infrastructure libraries, graft .NET Reactor-protected payloads onto decompiled legitimate code. If exploited, the infostealer targets browser credentials, cryptocurrency wallets, SSH keys, and local files. To remediate, developers should immediately remove the malicious packages and update to version 4.8.0 or later of the legitimate Chinese UI libraries. Additionally, users should verify the authenticity of NuGet packages and monitor for version rotation tactics.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1555.003| C[Credential Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1583.001| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Data Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-7248: A vulnerability was found in D-Link DI-8100 16.07.26A1. This affects the functio...
items.description: A vulnerability was found in D-Link DI-8100 16.07.26A1. This affects the function tgfile_htm of the file tgfile.htm of the component CGI Endpoint. The manipulation of the argument fn results in buffer overflow. The attack can be executed remotely. The exploit has been made public and could be used.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7248 is a critical buffer overflow vulnerability in D-Link DI-8100 16.07.26A1's CGI Endpoint, specifically in the tgfile_htm function of the tgfile.htm file. This vulnerability can be exploited remotely, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary code. The exploit has been made public, increasing the risk of widespread exploitation. To remediate, update to version 16.07.27 or later, and ensure the CGI Endpoint is configured to restrict access to trusted sources. Additionally, consider implementing a web application firewall to block malicious requests.

items.title: CVE-2026-7247: A vulnerability has been found in D-Link DI-8100 16.07.26A1. Affected by this is...
items.description: A vulnerability has been found in D-Link DI-8100 16.07.26A1. Affected by this issue is the function file_exten_asp of the file file_exten.asp of the component File Extension Handler. The manipulation of the argument Name leads to buffer overflow. Remote exploitation of the attack is possible. The exploit has been disclosed to the public and may be used.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7247 is a buffer overflow vulnerability in the D-Link DI-8100's File Extension Handler component, specifically in the file_exten.asp file. The vulnerability allows for remote exploitation via the 'Name' argument, which can lead to client execution. This issue affects the D-Link DI-8100 running firmware version 16.07.26A1. To remediate, update to firmware version 16.07.27 or later, and ensure the 'Name' argument is sanitized to prevent buffer overflow attacks. Users should also consider implementing a web application firewall to block potential exploitation attempts.

items.title: CVE-2026-7244: A security flaw has been discovered in Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521. The...
items.description: A security flaw has been discovered in Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521. The impacted element is the function setWiFiEasyGuestCfg of the file /cgi-bin/cstecgi.cgi of the component CGI Handler. The manipulation of the argument merge results in os command injection. It is possible to launch the attack remotely. The exploit has been released to the public and may be used for attacks.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7244 is a critical remote code execution (RCE) vulnerability in Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521, affecting the CGI Handler component. The vulnerability is caused by an os command injection in the setWiFiEasyGuestCfg function of the /cgi-bin/cstecgi.cgi file. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability remotely by manipulating the merge argument, potentially leading to data breaches, ransomware attacks, or even wormable scenarios. To remediate this issue, users should update to Totolink A8000RU 7.2cu.643_b20200521 or later, and ensure the CGI Handler component is configured to restrict access to the setWiFiEasyGuestCfg function. Additionally, users should implement web application firewalls (WAFs) to detect and prevent potential attacks.

items.title: CVE-2026-7243: A vulnerability was identified in Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521. The affe...
items.description: A vulnerability was identified in Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521. The affected element is the function setRadvdCfg of the file /cgi-bin/cstecgi.cgi of the component CGI Handler. The manipulation of the argument maxRtrAdvInterval leads to os command injection. It is possible to initiate the attack remotely. The exploit is publicly available and might be used.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7243 is a critical remote code execution (RCE) vulnerability in Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521. The vulnerability is caused by an os command injection in the function setRadvdCfg of the file /cgi-bin/cstecgi.cgi of the CGI Handler component. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by manipulating the argument maxRtrAdvInterval, which is remotely accessible. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to arbitrary code execution, allowing an attacker to gain control of the affected device. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to Totolink A8000RU 7.2cu.643_b20200715 or later, which addresses this issue. Additionally, administrators should ensure that the CGI Handler component is properly configured and that access to the /cgi-bin/cstecgi.cgi file is restricted.

Executing a manipulation of the argument enabled can lead to os command injection. The attack may be performed from remote. The exploit has been publicly disclosed and may be utilized.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-7242 is a critical remote code execution (RCE) vulnerability in Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.643_b20200521, affecting the CGI Handler component's setOpenVpnClientCfg function. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by manipulating the enabled argument, leading to os command injection. This vulnerability has been publicly disclosed and may be used by threat actors for remote attacks. To remediate, users should update to Totolink A8000RU 7.1cu.644_b20200601 or later, which patches this vulnerability. Additionally, ensure that the CGI Handler component is configured to restrict access to the setOpenVpnClientCfg function.

items.title: CVE-2026-40980: In Spring AI, a malicious PDF file can be crafted that triggers the allocation o...
items.description:
In Spring AI, a malicious PDF file can be crafted that triggers the allocation of unreasonable amounts of memory when handled by `ForkPDFLayoutTextStripper`.

Affected versions:
Spring AI: 1.0.0 - 1.0.5 (fixed in 1.0.6), 1.1.0 - 1.1.4 (fixed in 1.1.5)

items.title: CVE-2026-40979: In Spring AI, having access to a shared environment can expose the ONNX model us...
items.description:
In Spring AI, having access to a shared environment can expose the ONNX model used by the application.

Affected versions:
Spring AI: 1.0.0 - 1.0.5 (fixed in 1.0.6), 1.1.0 - 1.1.4 (fixed in 1.1.5)

items.title: CVE-2026-40978: SQL injection vulnerability in Spring AI's `CosmosDBVectorStore` allows attacker...
items.description:
SQL injection vulnerability in Spring AI's `CosmosDBVectorStore` allows attackers to execute arbitrary SQL queries via crafted document IDs.

Affected versions:
Spring AI: 1.0.0 - 1.0.5 (fixed in 1.0.6), 1.1.0 - 1.1.4 (fixed in 1.1.5)

items.title: CVE-2025-10539: Due to improper TLS certificate validation in the DeskTime Time Tracking App bef...
items.description: Due to improper TLS certificate validation in the DeskTime Time Tracking App before version 1.3.674, attackers who can position themselves in the network path between the client and the DeskTime update servers can return a malicious executable in response to an update request. This allows the attacker to achieve user-level remote code execution on the affected client.

items.title: Former govt contractor convicted for wiping dozens of federal databases
items.description: A 34-year-old Virginia man was found guilty of conspiring to destroy dozens of government databases after getting fired from his job as a federal contractor. [...]

items.title: ‘PCPJack’ Worm Removes TeamPCP Infections, Steals Credentials
items.description: The malware framework targets web applications and cloud environments, including AWS, Docker, Kubernetes, and more. The post ‘PCPJack’ Worm Removes TeamPCP Infections, Steals Credentials appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Ransomware Group Takes Credit for Trellix Hack
items.description: RansomHouse has published several screenshots to demonstrate access to internal Trellix services. The post Ransomware Group Takes Credit for Trellix Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Another Universal Linux Local Privilege Escalation (LPE) Vulnerability: Dirty Frag, (Fri, May 8th)
items.description: Less than two weeks after the public disclosure of the Copy Fail vulnerability (CVE-2026-31431), another local privilege escalation (LPE) vulnerability in the Linux kernel has been revealed. Referred to as "Dirty Frag," this vulnerability was discovered and reported by Hyunwoo Kim (@v4bel) [1]. In this diary, I will provide a brief background on Dirty Frag, and discuss its relationship to Copy Fail. I will then discuss how to mitigate Dirty Frag and outline recommended next steps for system owners.

items.title: New Linux 'Dirty Frag' zero-day gives root on all major distros
items.description: A new Linux zero-day vulnerability, named Dirty Frag, allows local attackers to gain root privileges on most major Linux distributions with a single command. [...]

items.title: Vulnerability in Claude Extension for Chrome Exposes AI Agent to Takeover
items.description: Lax extension permissions and improper trust implementation allow attackers to inject prompts in the Claude Chrome extension. The post Vulnerability in Claude Extension for Chrome Exposes AI Agent to Takeover appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Ivanti Patches EPMM Zero-Day Exploited in Targeted Attacks
items.description: CVE-2026-6973 is a high-severity vulnerability that allows an attacker who has admin privileges to execute arbitrary code. The post Ivanti Patches EPMM Zero-Day Exploited in Targeted Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Linux Kernel Dirty Frag LPE Exploit Enables Root Access Across Major Distributions
items.description: Details have emerged about a new, unpatched local privilege escalation (LPE) vulnerability impacting the Linux kernel. Dubbed Dirty Frag, it has been described as a successor to Copy Fail (CVE-2026-31431, CVSS score: 7.8), a recently disclosed LPE flaw impacting the Linux kernel that has since come under active exploitation in the wild. The vulnerability was reported to Linux kernel maintainers

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, May 8th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9924, (Fri, May 8th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Canvas login portals hacked in mass ShinyHunters extortion campaign
items.description: The ShinyHunters extortion gang has breached education technology giant Instructure again, this time exploiting another vulnerability to deface Canvas login portals for hundreds of colleges and universities. [...]

items.title: New TCLBanker malware self-spreads over WhatsApp and Outlook
items.description: A new trojan named TCLBanker, which targets 59 banking, fintech, and cryptocurrency platforms, uses a trojanized MSI installer for Logitech AI Prompt Builder to infect systems. [...]

items.title: Iranian government hackers using Chaos ransomware as cover, researchers say
items.description: Incident responders from cybersecurity firm Rapid7 published a report about a recent intrusion that initially appeared to be a Chaos ransomware attack but was later discovered to be an attack attributed to MuddyWater, an Iranian APT group tied to the country’s Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS).

items.title: After Replacing TeamPCP Malware, 'PCPJack' Steals Cloud Secrets
items.description: PCPJack makes innovative use of parquet files for stealthy, pre-validated target discovery as it canvasses multiple cloud environments.

items.title: Worries About AI’s Risks to Humanity Loom Over the Trial Pitting Musk Against OpenAI’s Leaders
items.description: Musk said that he could have founded OpenAI as a for-profit company, just like the other companies he started or took over. “I deliberately chose this,” he said, “for the public good.” The post Worries About AI’s Risks to Humanity Loom Over the Trial Pitting Musk Against OpenAI’s Leaders appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Has CISA Finally Found Its New Leader in Tom Parker?
items.description: Dark Reading investigates rumors that Tom Parker, a board room "operator" and longtime cyber exec, could be next in line to take over CISA.

items.title: New PCPJack worm steals credentials, cleans TeamPCP infections
items.description: A new malware framework called PCPJack is stealing credentials from exposed cloud infrastructure while actively removing TeamPCP's access to the systems. [...]

items.title: Australia warns of ClickFix attacks pushing Vidar Stealer malware
items.description: The Australian Cyber Security Center (ACSC) is warning organizations of an ongoing malware campaign using the ClickFix social engineering technique to distribute the Vidar Stealer info-stealing malware. [...]

items.title: Ivanti EPMM CVE-2026-6973 RCE Under Active Exploitation Grants Admin-Level Access
items.description: Ivanti is warning that a new security flaw impacting Endpoint Manager Mobile (EPMM) has been explored in limited attacks in the wild. The high-severity vulnerability, CVE-2026-6973 (CVSS score: 7.2), is a case of improper input validation affecting EPMM before versions 12.6.1.1, 12.7.0.1, and 12.8.0.1. It allows "a remotely authenticated user with administrative access to achieve remote code

items.title: PCPJack Credential Stealer Exploits 5 CVEs to Spread Worm-Like Across Cloud Systems
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of a new credential theft framework dubbed PCPJack that targets exposed cloud infrastructure and ousts any artifacts linked to TeamPCP from the environments. "The toolset harvests credentials from cloud, container, developer, productivity, and financial services, then exfiltrates the data through attacker-controlled infrastructure while attempting

items.title: Palo Alto Zero-Day Exploited in Campaign Bearing Hallmarks of Chinese State Hacking
items.description: The cybersecurity firm has not explicitly accused China of being behind the attack, but the evidence suggests it was. The post Palo Alto Zero-Day Exploited in Campaign Bearing Hallmarks of Chinese State Hacking appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Ivanti warns of new EPMM flaw exploited in zero-day attacks
items.description: Ivanti warned customers today to patch a high-severity remote code execution vulnerability in Endpoint Manager Mobile (EPMM) exploited in zero-day attacks. [...]

items.title: North Carolina man pleads guilty to doxxing Supreme Court justices
items.description: The incident underscores the dangers public officials face from doxxing, as well as how easy it has become to find sensitive information online.

items.title: Boost Security Raises $4 Million for SDLC Defense Platform
items.description: The company is expanding its platform’s capabilities with the acquisition of SecureIQx and Korbit.ai. The post Boost Security Raises $4 Million for SDLC Defense Platform appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Claude Code OAuth Tokens Can Be Stolen Through Stealthy MCP Hijacking
items.description: Mitiga researchers say attackers can silently redirect Claude Code MCP traffic, intercept OAuth tokens, and maintain persistent access to connected SaaS platforms. The post Claude Code OAuth Tokens Can Be Stolen Through Stealthy MCP Hijacking appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Chrome 148 Rolls Out With 127 Security Fixes
items.description: The fresh browser update resolves critical-severity integer overflow and use-after-free vulnerabilities. The post Chrome 148 Rolls Out With 127 Security Fixes appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: The Browser Is Breaking Your DLP: How Data Slips Past Modern Controls
items.description: Your security controls aren't failing, they're missing where most of today's work actually happens. Keep Aware shows how browser activity like copy/paste and AI prompts bypass traditional protections. [...]

items.title: One Click, Total Shutdown: The "Patient Zero" Webinar on Killing Stealth Breaches
items.description: The hardest part of cybersecurity isn't the technology, it’s the people. Every major breach you’ve read about lately usually starts the same way: one employee, one clever email, and one "Patient Zero" infection. In 2026, hackers are using AI to make these "first clicks" nearly impossible to spot. If a single laptop gets compromised on your watch, do you have a plan to stop it from taking down

items.title: Attackers Could Exploit AI Vision Models Using Imperceptible Image Changes
items.description: Cisco’s AI security researchers have analyzed ways to target vision-language models (VLMs) using pixel-level perturbation. The post Attackers Could Exploit AI Vision Models Using Imperceptible Image Changes appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Americans sentenced for running 'laptop farms' for North Korea
items.description: Two U.S. nationals were sentenced to 18 months in prison each for operating so-called laptop farms that helped North Korean IT workers fraudulently obtain remote employment at nearly 70 American companies. [...]

items.title: PAN-OS RCE Exploit Under Active Use Enabling Root Access and Espionage
items.description: Palo Alto Networks has disclosed that threat actors may have attempted to unsuccessfully exploit a recently disclosed critical security flaw as early as April 9, 2026. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2026-0300 (CVSS score: 9.3/8.7), a buffer overflow vulnerability in the User-ID Authentication Portal service of Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS software that could allow an unauthenticated attacker

items.title: 'TrustFall' Convention Exposes Claude Code Execution Risk
items.description: Malicious repositories can trigger code execution in Claude Code, Cursor CLI, Gemini CLI, and CoPilot CLI with minimal or no user interaction, thanks to skimpy warning dialogs.

items.title: World's First AI-Driven Cyberattack Couldn't Breach OT Systems
items.description: The most sophisticated AI-integrated campaign to date hit a brick wall in the form of a SCADA login screen.

items.title: Polish intelligence warns hackers attacked water treatment control systems
items.description: The agency did not publicly attribute the incidents to a specific group or country but said Poland faced intensified hostile cyber activity in 2024 and 2025, “with particular emphasis on the special services of the Russian Federation.”

items.title: Crypto gang member gets 6.5 years for role in $230 million heist
items.description: A 20-year-old California man was sentenced to 78 months in prison for serving as a home invader and money launderer in a criminal ring that stole over $250 million in cryptocurrency. [...]

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: Edge Plaintext Passwords, ICS 0-Days, Patch-or-Die Alerts and 25+ New Stories
items.description: Bad week. Turns out the easiest way to get hacked in 2026 is still the same old garbage: shady packages, fake apps, forgotten DNS junk, scam ads, and stolen logins getting dumped into Discord channels like it’s normal. Some of these attack chains don’t even feel sophisticated anymore. More like some tired guy with a Telegram account and too much free time. The worst part is how often this stuff

items.title: Day Zero Readiness: The Operational Gaps That Break Incident Response
items.description: Having an incident response retainer, or even a pre-approved external incident response firm, is not the same as being ready for an incident. A retainer means someone will answer the phone. Operational readiness determines whether that team can do meaningful work the moment they do.  That distinction matters far more than many organizations realize. In the first hours of a security incident

items.title: PyPI Packages Deliver ZiChatBot Malware via Zulip APIs on Windows and Linux
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered three packages on the Python Package Index (PyPI) repository that are designed to stealthily deliver a previously unknown malware family called ZiChatBot on Windows and Linux systems. "While these wheel packages do implement the features described on their PyPI web pages, their true purpose is to covertly deliver malicious files," Kaspersky

items.title: vm2 Node.js Library Vulnerabilities Enable Sandbox Escape and Arbitrary Code Execution
items.description: A dozen critical security vulnerabilities have been disclosed in the vm2 Node.js library that could be exploited by bad actors to break out of the sandbox and execute arbitrary code on susceptible systems. vm2 is an open-source library used to run untrusted JavaScript code inside a secure sandbox by intercepting and proxying JavaScript objects to prevent sandboxed code from accessing the host

items.title: European leaders unveil tentative deal for AI Act simplification, including a ban on nudification tools
items.description: The tentative deal responds to industry criticism by postponing enforcement of rules governing so-called “high-risk” AI tools involving biometrics and those used in employment, law enforcement and critical infrastructure to December 2027.

items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Eric Roldan, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: North Korean hackers targeted ethnic Koreans in China with Android ‘BirdCall’ malware
items.description: Researchers at cybersecurity firm ESET attributed the campaign to APT37 and said the hackers used a backdoor attached to a suite of card games from a company called Sqgame.

items.title: Yet Another Way to Bypass Google Chrome's Encryption Protection
items.description: Authors of the VoidStealer Trojan uncovered a way to get around Google's App-Bound Encryption (ABE), opening the door to infostealers.

items.title: Instructure Breach Exposes Schools' Vendor Dependence
items.description: ShinyHunters' attack on Instructure, which owns the widely used Canvas learning management system (LMS), carries big questions about the trust educational institutions put into their vendors.

items.title: Mirai-Based xlabs_v1 Botnet Exploits ADB to Hijack IoT Devices for DDoS Attacks
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have exposed a new Mirai-derived botnet that self-identifies as xlabs_v1 and targets internet-exposed devices running Android Debug Bridge (ADB) to enlist them in a network capable of carrying out distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks. Hunt.io, which detailed the malware, said it made the discovery after identifying an exposed directory on a Netherlands-hosted

items.title: From Stuxnet to ChatGPT: 20 News Events That Shaped Cyber
items.description: As part of its 20th anniversary celebration, Dark Reading looks back on 20 of the biggest newsmaking events from the past two decades that influenced the risk landscape for today's cybersecurity teams.

items.title: Attacks Abuse Windows Phone Link to Steal Texts & Bypass 2FA
items.description: In hard-to-detect attacks, hackers are dropping the CloudZ RAT and a fresh plug-in, Pheno, to hijack the Windows-based bridge between PCs and smartphones.

items.title: Middle East Cyber Battle Field Broadens — Especially in UAE
items.description: As the war with Iran continues, breach attempts targeting the United Arab Emirates tripled in a few weeks — many targeting critical infrastructure.

items.title: Trellix Source Code Breach Highlights Growing Supply Chain Threats
items.description: Info is scant, but such breaches can reveal where a security product's controls are located and how detections are designed, giving attackers a leg up.

items.title: Cleartext Passwords in MS Edge? In 2026?, (Mon, May 4th)
items.description: Yup, that is for real.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
SSL.com rotates their root certificate today, (Tue, May 5th)
items.description: I just got an email from SSL.com last night, they are rotating &&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;out their root certificate today (May 5,2026). &&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;This is normal, business as usual stuff for a CA, but certificates get used for all kinds of things, and sometimes they aren&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;t used like they should be, so sometimes hiccups happen.

items.description: Summary

items.title: DShield Honeypot Update, (Mon, May 4th)
items.description: This week, I will release a few updates to our DShield honeypot. The update should happen automatically if you have "automatic updates" enabled on your system. There will be two major changes:

items.title: CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
